m_Item1.QuestionText: Who introduced Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: Git was originally authored by Linus Torvalds in 2005 for development of the Linux kernel, with other kernel developers contributing to its initial development. Since 2005, Junio Hamano has been the core maintainer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When was Git Initial released?
m_Item1.Explanation: Git was initially released on 7 April 2005 by Linus Torvalds, the creator of Linux, in response to the need for a distributed version control system after the Linux community parted ways with BitKeeper. It has since become the standard tool for source code management in modern software development.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: Git is a distributed version control system (VCS) designed to handle everything from small to very large projects with speed and efficiency. It allows developers to Track changes in source code over time, Collaborate with others without overwriting each other's work, Revert to previous versions if needed, Branch and merge code for parallel development. It is not a nickname for GitHub (which is a hosting service for Git repositories), a programming language, or a remote repository platform on its own.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the command to get the installed version of Git?
m_Item1.Explanation:
To check the installed version of Git on your system, you use: git --version.
This command outputs something like: git version 2.42.0

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which option should you use to set the default user name for every repository on your computer?

Options.OptionText: No need to specify, that is the default.

m_Item1.Explanation:
To set the default user name for every Git repository on your computer, you use the --global flag with the git config command:
git config --global user.name "Your Name".
This stores the configuration in your global Git settings, applying it to all repositories unless overridden locally. The other options are either invalid or incorrect for this purpose.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What command lets you create a connection between a local and remote repository?
m_Item1.Explanation:
git remote add origin

This command establishes a link between your local Git repository and a remote repository (typically hosted on platforms like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket). origin is the default name given to the remote repository.The full command usually looks as below
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo.git
After running this command, your local repository will have a connection to the remote repository, and you can use Git commands to push and pull changes between them. The origin is a conventional name for the default remote repository, but you can choose a different name if desired.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true when you use the following command: git add -A

Options.OptionText: All new and updated files are staged
Files are staged in alphabetical order

m_Item1.Explanation:
The command git add -A stages all changes in your working directory, including: 
New files (untracked), Modified files, Deleted files. It ensures that every change—whether it's an addition, modification, or deletion—is included in the next commit. This makes it more comprehensive than git add . or git add -u, which have narrower scopes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What option can you use to apply git configurations across your entire git environment?
m_Item1.Explanation: The --global option ensures that the configuration is applied globally to your user account, affecting all Git repositories on your machine. These specific commands are setting the global configuration for the user's name and email.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A Git tree object_____

Options.OptionText: can only contain references to blobs
can only contain references to other tree objects
can contain references to other tree objects or blobs
can contain file data or references to other tree objects or blobs

m_Item1.Explanation:
In Git, a tree object represents a directory. It can contain:
References to blob objects: which represent file contents.References to other tree objects: which represent subdirectories.
This structure allows Git to model a complete file system hierarchy. Tree objects do not contain actual file data themselves—they only point to blobs and other trees.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a valid option for 'credential.helper' setting?
m_Item1.Explanation:
The credential.helper setting in Git is used to configure how Git stores and retrieves credentials. Valid options include: 
store: Saves credentials in plain text on disk. cache: Temporarily stores credentials in memory for a configurable time. no value (default): Prompts for credentials every time unless a helper is configured.However, temporary is not a recognized credential helper in Git, making it the invalid option.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the full form of GIT?
m_Item1.Explanation: Git is a distributed version control system that helps developers track changes in their codebase, collaborate efficiently, and manage different versions of their projects. It was originally created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 for the development of the Linux kernel.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which Command is used to show limited number of commits?
m_Item1.Explanation: This command displays the last n commits in the Git log. For example, git log -5 will show the most recent 5 commits.

m_Item1.QuestionText: "Sparse checkout" allows you to________

Options.OptionText: init only the main submodule of the repository
check out a branch from the repository without some of its commits
clone a remote such that you get a working copy but no repository (no .git folder)
check out only some files from the repository into your working copy

m_Item1.Explanation: Sparse checkout is a Git feature that lets you selectively populate your working directory with only a subset of files from the repository. This is especially useful when working with large repositories where you only need a few specific directories or files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the command to set the user email for the current repository?
m_Item1.Explanation:
This command sets the email address for the current Git repository. To apply it, you would run:
git config user.email "your_email@example.com"

If you want to set it globally (for all repositories), you can add the --global flag:
git config --global user.email "your_email@example.com"

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the command to add all files and changes of the current folder to the staging environment of the Git repository?
m_Item1.Explanation:
This command stages all changes in the current folder — including new files, modified files, and deletions — for the next commit.Alternatively, you can also use:
git add .

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the command to get the current status of the Git repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command git status shows the current state of the working directory and the staging area. It lets you see which changes have been staged, which haven’t, and which files aren’t being tracked by Git. Here’s what it typically displays: Changes to be committed, Changes not staged for commit, Untracked files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the command to initialize Git on the current repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command git init initializes a new Git repository in the current directory. It creates a .git folder that tracks all version control information for your project.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Git commit history is automatically deleted:

Options.OptionText: Commit history is never automatically deleted.

m_Item1.Explanation: Considering its significance for the team Commit history is never automatically deleted. Git does not automatically delete commit history. However, commits that are no longer referenced by any branch or tag (such as those removed via git reset or git rebase) may eventually be cleaned up by Git's garbage collection (git gc). This process is not on a fixed schedule and only affects unreachable commits. So unless you explicitly remove commits or rewrite history, your commit history remains intact.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which check summing technique is used by Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: Git uses the SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm 1) hashing algorithm to identify and track objects like commits, trees, and blobs. Each object is assigned a unique 40-character hexadecimal string — its SHA-1 hash — which acts as a fingerprint for the content. Although SHA-1 has known vulnerabilities, Git has implemented additional safeguards and is gradually transitioning to more secure algorithms like SHA-256 in newer versions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What's a shortcut to staging all the changes you have?
m_Item1.Explanation:
To stage all changes (including new, modified, and deleted files), you can use:
 git add . or git add --all 
Both commands will prepare all changes in your working directory for the next commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you add a commit message to a commit?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command Git commit -m "I'm coding!" creates a new commit with the message "I'm coding!". The -m flag allows you to include the commit message directly in the command line, which is a quick and common way to document your changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of version control?

Options.OptionText: Test all external packages for compatibility.
Track new and changed features of the product.
Limit the code to a particular programming language.
Archive code and ensure that changes to the code can be tracked.

m_Item1.Explanation:
Archive code and ensure that changes to the code can be tracked.
Version control, also known as source code control or revision control, is a system that allows developers to track changes to code over time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the use of Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: Git is a distributed version control system (VCS) used to: Track changes in source code during software development Collaborate across teams by managing branches and merges, Maintain history of every change, enabling rollbacks and audits Support CI/CD pipelines by integrating with tools like Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and GitLab. It is not a containerization, CI, or monitoring tool—but it plays a foundational role in enabling those processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the command git pull do?

Options.OptionText: Uploads changes to the remote repository
Downloads changes from the remote repository

m_Item1.Explanation:
git pull is a combination of two commands: 
git fetch: Retrieves changes from the remote repository and git merge: Integrates those changes into your current branch. So when you run git pull, it downloads and merges updates from the remote branch into your local working directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the command git commit -m "message"?

Options.OptionText: Adds a new file to the repository
Commits changes to the local repository with a message
Deletes a file from the repository

m_Item1.Explanation: The command: git commit -m "message" is used to record staged changes in your local Git repository. The -m flag lets you include a commit message that describes the changes, helping you and others understand the purpose of that commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Git, what is the purpose of the .gitignore file?

Options.OptionText: To exclude files and directories from version control
To track changes in the project

m_Item1.Explanation:
The .gitignore file is used to specify files and directories that should be ignored by Git. 

The .gitignore file tells Git which files or directories to ignore — meaning they won’t be tracked, staged, or committed. This is useful for keeping out: Temporary files (e.g., .log, .tmp), Build artifacts (e.g., dist/, bin/), Sensitive data (e.g., .env, API keys), OS-specific clutter (e.g., .DS_Store, Thumbs.db). 
Each line in .gitignore specifies a pattern to match files or folders.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of a Version Control System (VCS) in CI/CD?

Options.OptionText: Storing and managing source code changes

m_Item1.Explanation: A Version Control System (VCS) is used to store and manage source code changes, enabling collaboration and version tracking. A VCS like Git is the foundation of any CI/CD pipeline. It  Tracks changes to source code over time, Enables collaboration among developers, Triggers CI/CD workflows when code is pushed or merged, Maintains history for rollback, auditing, and debugging.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which chart depicts the amount of work remaining in the project?
m_Item1.Explanation: A burn-up chart shows work completed over time, often alongside the total scope. While it also tracks progress, it focuses more on what’s been done rather than what’s left. So, while both are useful, only the burn-down chart directly depicts the amount of work remaining

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command initializes a new Git repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: The git init command is used to initialize a new Git repository in your current directory. It creates a hidden .git folder that tracks all version control information.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command adds changes to the staging area in Git?
m_Item1.Explanation:
The git add command is used to stage changes in your working directory, preparing them for the next commit. It tells Git which changes you want to include.
git add filename.txt
Or to stage all changes: git add .

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to clone a remote repository?
m_Item1.Explanation:
The git clone command is used to create a local copy of a remote repository. It downloads all the files, branches, and commit history from the remote source. 
Example usage: git clone https://github.com/username/repository.git
The command above This sets up the local repository with a connection to the remote, allowing you to pull, push, and collaborate. The other options listed are either invalid or incorrectly formatted.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you view the differences between the working directory and the last commit?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command git diff shows the differences between your working directory and the last commit. It helps you See what changes have been made but not yet staged, Review modifications before committing, Understand how your current work differs from the repository’s history.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a primary benefit of using a code versioning system?

Options.OptionText: Minimizing the use of server resources

m_Item1.Explanation: A code versioning system allows multiple developers to collaborate by tracking changes and managing modifications to the project's codebase over time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of version control systems, what is a 'commit'?

Options.OptionText: A package of code ready for production
A snapshot of changes recorded in the repository
A method for merging code branches

m_Item1.Explanation: A 'commit' in a version control system is a saved snapshot of changes made to the project, representing a point in the code's history.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a typical feature of distributed version control systems (DVCS), such as Git?

Options.OptionText: Each user has a local copy of the repository
Centralized control of versions and changes
Ability to work offline with full local history

m_Item1.Explanation: Unlike centralized systems, DVCS does not rely on a single server to store all versions of a project's files. Instead, each user has their full copy of the repository, which allows for decentralized management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What was the primary reason for the development of the early version control systems like SCCS (Source Code Control System)?

Options.OptionText: To manage and track changes in the source code files.

m_Item1.Explanation: The early version control systems like SCCS were developed primarily to manage and track the changes made to source code files, improving collaboration among developers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following early version control systems was developed by Walter F. Tichy?
m_Item1.Explanation: Walter F. Tichy developed the Revision Control System (RCS) in the early 1980s to replace SCCS and provide improved version control facilities.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which version control system paved the way for many modern systems by introducing the concept of a centralized repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: CVS (Concurrent Versions System) introduced the concept of a centralized repository, which greatly influenced the design of many subsequent version control systems, including Subversion.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following version control systems is primarily distributed and was developed by Linus Torvalds?
m_Item1.Explanation: Git is a distributed version control system developed by Linus Torvalds, emphasizing speed, data integrity, and support for distributed, non-linear workflows.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main advantage of using a distributed version control system like Mercurial over a centralized system like CVS?

Options.OptionText: Repositories can be cloned completely, providing more flexibility in branching and merging

m_Item1.Explanation: Distributed version control systems like Mercurial allow users to clone entire repositories, making it easier to work offline and providing greater flexibility with independent branching and merging.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary function of a version control system like Git?

Options.OptionText: To track changes in source code during software development

m_Item1.Explanation: A version control system like Git is primarily used to track changes in source code over time, allowing developers to collaborate, revert to previous states, and manage code history.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes a Git commit?

Options.OptionText: A request to join a project repository
A record of changes made to the files in the repository
A clone of the remote repository
A command to initialize a new repository

m_Item1.Explanation: A commit in Git is a snapshot of the changes made to the files in the repository, serving as a save point that records the current state.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'git clone' command do?

Options.OptionText: Creates a new branch in the Git repository
Downloads a copy of a repository and its history
Adds changes to the staging area

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git clone' command is used to create a local copy of a remote repository and its entire history, making it available for local development.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a widely used Git command?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'git send' is not a standard Git command. Common Git commands include 'git pull', 'git push', and 'git merge'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Git, what is a 'branch'?

Options.OptionText: A security feature that prevents unauthorized access to a repository
A command used to merge multiple files into a single file
A separate line of development within a repository
A system that automatically builds and tests the code

m_Item1.Explanation: A branch in Git is a separate line of development, enabling developers to work on features or fixes independently before merging changes with the main project.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the function of the 'git init' command in Git?

Options.OptionText: To start a new branch in a repository
To create a new local Git repository
To initialize a remote Git repository

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git init' command is used to initialize a new, empty local Git repository within a directory. It sets up all the necessary files and folders to start versioning.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to record the changes made to the repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git commit' command is used to save or record changes made to the repository, along with a message describing the changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What purpose does the '.gitignore' file serve in a Git repository?

Options.OptionText: To specify which files and directories to ignore in the repository
To keep track of all commit messages
To list all branches in use
To specify the default branch to use

m_Item1.Explanation: The '.gitignore' file is used to specify files and directories that Git should ignore, preventing unnecessary files from being included in commits.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following describes the difference between 'git merge' and 'git rebase'?

Options.OptionText: Both commands are used interchangeably to combine branches
git merge combines the histories of branches while git rebase reapplies commits on top of another base branch
git rebase deletes a branch, git merge creates a tag
git merge creates a backup, git rebase permanently deletes files

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git merge' command combines the histories of branches by adding a merge commit, whereas 'git rebase' applies commits from one branch onto another, effectively rewriting the project history.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you add changes from all files in the working directory to the staging area using a single command?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command 'git add .' stages all changes from the current directory upwards, preparing them to be committed. It is a shorthand for staging changes from every file in the working directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Git, what is an untracked file?

Options.OptionText: A file that is being tracked by Git for changes
A file that has been staged but not yet committed
A file that has not been added to the staging area
A file that is in the staging area but contains uncommitted changes

m_Item1.Explanation: An untracked file is a file that has not been added to the staging area and hence is not being tracked by Git for changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command can be used to view all tracked files in the current repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git status' command provides the current state of the working directory and staging area, listing all tracked and untracked files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'git add' command do to untracked files?

Options.OptionText: Moves them to the staging area to be tracked

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git add' command adds untracked files to the staging area, making them tracked and part of the next commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you want to ignore specific untracked files without deleting them, what should you do?

Options.OptionText: Remove them from the working directory
Commit them with a special message

m_Item1.Explanation: To ignore specific untracked files without deleting them, you should list them in the .gitignore file within the repository. The .gitignore file tells Git to ignore specific files or directories, meaning Git will not track them even if they exist in your working directory. This is ideal for Temporary files, Build artifacts, IDE settings, Sensitive configuration files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of using 'git commit' in a Git workflow?

Options.OptionText: To clone a repository to the local machine
To save changes to the staging area
To move changes from the staging area to the local repository
To fetch the latest changes from the remote repository

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git commit' command is used to move the changes from the staging area to the local repository, creating a snapshot of the project's current state.  In Git, the git commit command takes the changes you've staged (using git add) and records them in your local repository as a new commit. This creates a snapshot of your project at that point in time, complete with a unique ID and a message describing the changes

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which flag would you use with 'git commit' to amend the last commit?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '--amend' flag is used with 'git commit' to modify the last commit, allowing you to update the commit message or the content of the commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the '-m' flag do when used with 'git commit'?

Options.OptionText: It merges the commit into the main branch
It adds a manual annotation to the commit
It allows you to provide a commit message directly from the command line
It moves the commit to the remote repository

m_Item1.Explanation: The '-m' flag allows you to specify a commit message directly in the command line, making it quicker to commit changes with a succinct explanation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following commands can be used to view the history of commits?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git log' command is used to view the commit history, showing the chronological series of commits made to the repository, along with details about each commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main purpose of creating a branch in Git?

Options.OptionText: To permanently delete files from the repository
To create an independent line of development
To duplicate the entire repository for backup

m_Item1.Explanation: A branch in Git allows developers to diverge from the main line and develop features independently without affecting the main project.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to create a new branch in Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command 'git branch new-branch' creates a new branch named 'new-branch' in the current repository.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command should you use to switch to an existing branch in Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: To switch to another branch, you use 'git checkout branch-name', although recent versions of Git recommend using 'git switch branch-name' instead.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you list all branches in your current Git repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git branch --list' command displays all branches in the current repository, highlighting the current branch with an asterisk.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a fast-forward merge in Git?

Options.OptionText: A simple merge where the branch pointer is moved forward
A special type of branch created for merging

m_Item1.Explanation: In a fast-forward merge, if there are no divergent commits, the branch pointer simply moves forward to the latest commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scenarios would NOT allow a fast-forward merge?

Options.OptionText: The target branch is ahead of the current branch
There are no new commits in the current branch
The two branches have been merged previously

m_Item1.Explanation: A fast-forward merge cannot occur when the branches have divergent histories as it requires linear, non-conflicting commits.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to merge a specified branch into the current branch?
m_Item1.Explanation: This command merges the specified branch (e.g., branch) into your current branch. For example, if you're on main and you want to merge changes from feature, you'd run: git merge feature

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens during a 'three-way merge' in Git?

Options.OptionText: Git uses three branches to perform the merge operation
Git combines three commits into one
Git resolves divergent histories using a common ancestor commit
Git merges three repositories into one

m_Item1.Explanation: A three-way merge in Git occurs when two branches have diverged — meaning both have new commits since they split from a common ancestor. Git uses this common ancestor, along with the tips of the two branches being merged, to intelligently combine the changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of 'git stash' in Git?

Options.OptionText: To temporarily save changes not ready to be committed
To create a new branch from the current commit
To merge changes from two different branches

m_Item1.Explanation: 'git stash' is used to temporarily set aside changes in the working directory that are not ready to be committed, allowing you to switch branches or work on something else without committing unready changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: After using 'git stash', what command would you use to retrieve the most recent stashed changes?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'git stash pop' applies the most recent stashed changes back to your working directory and removes the stash from the stash list.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you list all the stashed changes saved in your Git repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'git stash list' is used to display a list of all stashed changes, including their identifiers and commit messages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the 'git status' command in Git?

Options.OptionText: To display the current branch and its commit hashes
To show the state of the working directory and staging area
To initialize a new Git repository

m_Item1.Explanation: 'git status' is used to display the current state of the working directory and the staging area, helping you understand which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git yet.

m_Item1.QuestionText: After running 'git status', the output shows that a file is in the 'Untracked files' section. What does this mean?

Options.OptionText: The file is tracked but has been modified since the last commit
The file is being tracked and has no changes
The file is not tracked by Git and will not be included in commits
The file has conflicts that need to be resolved

m_Item1.Explanation: An 'Untracked file' in 'git status' indicates that the file exists in the working directory but is not being tracked by Git yet, meaning it will not be included in future commits unless added.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you see a message saying 'Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'' after running 'git status', what does it imply?

Options.OptionText: There are uncommitted changes in the working directory
The local branch has no changes compared to the remote branch
There are staged changes waiting to be committed
The local branch contains changes not yet pushed to the remote

m_Item1.Explanation: The message 'Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'' means that the local branch matches the remote branch exactly, with no additional local or remote changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command in Git is used to view the commit history along with the changes made in each commit?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git log --name-status' command is used to display not only the commit history but also the names of files that were changed, added, or deleted in each commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'git reflog' command display?

Options.OptionText: History of any reference (branch and HEAD) modifications

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git reflog' command is used to show a history of all the changes made to the references (branches, HEAD, etc.) in Git, including those that are not visible through 'git log'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Git, what does the command 'git log --oneline' do?

Options.OptionText: Shows a summary of the commit history on one line per commit
Displays a detailed history of all commits and changes
Lists the branches and their history

m_Item1.Explanation:
The 'git log --oneline' command is a shorthand version of 'git log', displaying each commit in a concise, one-line format that includes the commit hash and commit message. The git log --oneline command provides a condensed view of the commit history, displaying each commit as a single line that includes: The abbreviated commit hash, The commit message

This format is especially useful for quickly scanning through the history or identifying specific commits

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which git command do you use to update your local repository with changes from the remote repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git fetch' command is used to download objects and refs from another repository. It updates your remote-tracking branches and prepares your local repository to be merged with changes from the remote repository.

m_Item1.QuestionText: After using 'git fetch', which command should you run to incorporate changes into your local branch?
m_Item1.Explanation: After fetching the latest changes from the remote repository, 'git merge' can be used to merge those changes into your local branch. After running git fetch, Git downloads changes from the remote repository but does not apply them to your current branch. To incorporate those changes, you need to merge the fetched updates manually:

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command allows you to directly update your working directory with changes from the remote branch and also merge them?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git pull' command is used to fetch and download content from a remote repository and immediately update the local repository to match that content, merging any changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the difference between 'git fetch' and 'git pull'?

Options.OptionText: git fetch only updates remote branch pointers whereas git pull also merges changes
git fetch deletes remote branch pointers, git pull does not
git fetch saves changes on the remote branch and git pull does not
Both commands are exactly the same

m_Item1.Explanation:
The 'git fetch' command downloads new data but doesn't integrate it into your working files, while
'git pull' downloads data and immediately integrates it into the working repository by merging.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which git command would you use to list changes that haven't been committed yet?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git diff' command in Git is used to display the differences between files and can show changes that have not been staged yet. This command shows the changes in your working directory that have not yet been staged or committed. It’s perfect for reviewing what’s been modified before you decide to add or commit those changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you want to see changes between your working directory and the index, which command should you use?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git diff' command displays the difference between files in the index and your last commit, showing changes that are staged for the next commit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you view the changes committed to the HEAD but not yet pushed to the remote repository?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'git log origin/main..HEAD' shows the commits in HEAD that are not in origin/main, indicating the differences that have not been pushed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to create a new branch named 'feature' in Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command 'git branch feature' is used to create a new branch named 'feature' in the current repository. This command creates a new branch named feature but does not switch to it. If you want to create and switch to the new branch immediately, you can use: git checkout -b feature

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you create and switch to a new branch named 'development' in one command?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git checkout -b development' command creates a new branch named 'development' and checks it out in one step.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you want to make changes on a new branch while keeping the current branch intact, which command should you use?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command 'git checkout -b new-feature' creates a new branch called new-feature and switches to it immediately, allowing you to make changes there while keeping the current branch untouched.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the command 'git branch' do if used without any additional options?
m_Item1.Explanation: Executing 'git branch' without options lists all the local branches in the repository. When you run git branch without any additional options, it displays a list of all local branches in your repository. The currently checked-out branch will be highlighted with an asterisk (*).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following commands would NOT create a new branch in Git?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command 'git branch -d new-branch' is used to delete a branch named 'new-branch', not to create one. This command does not create a new branch — it actually deletes the branch named new-branch if it exists.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to merge a branch named 'feature-branch' into the current branch in Git?
m_Item1.Explanation:
This command merges the changes from the feature-branch into your current branch. Before running it, make sure you're checked out to the branch you want to merge into (typically main or develop).
git checkout main
git merge feature-branch
This will integrate the changes from feature-branch into main.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'git merge --abort' command?

Options.OptionText: It cancels the current merge process and cleans up the merge state.
It completes the merge process by automatically resolving conflicts.
It lists files that are conflicting during a merge.
It saves the merge progress temporarily.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git merge --abort' command is used to stop the merge process and return the repository to the state before the merge began, thus cancelling the merge.

m_Item1.QuestionText: During a merge in Git, which option will help you better understand the differences between branches?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'git diff' command shows the differences between two branches, helping you to understand what will be merged or what changed between commits.

m_Item1.QuestionText: After resolving merge conflicts manually, which command should you use to mark them as resolved?
m_Item1.Explanation: After manually resolving merge conflicts in the affected files, you use git add to mark those files as resolved. This stages the changes and tells Git that the conflicts have been handled.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the effect of the 'git merge --no-ff' option?

Options.OptionText: It prevents fast-forwarding during a merge and creates an explicit merge commit.
It generates a fast-forward merge without conflicts.
It automatically deletes the merged branch.
It forces conflicts to be resolved automatically.

m_Item1.Explanation: Using git merge --no-ff ensures that Git creates a merge commit, even if the merge could be resolved with a fast-forward. This is useful for preserving the history of feature branches, making it clear when a branch was merged and keeping the project history more structured.
